question:
You have a container with 1 kilogram of pure distilled water at room temperature (25°C) and normal atmospheric pressure (1 atm). The water contains no dissolved salts, minerals, or other substances - just H₂O molecules. What is the pH of this pure water system at 25°C?

a) 7.500  
b) 7.000  
c) 6.997  
d) 6.500

System Description: You have a container with 1 kilogram of pure distilled water at 25°C (1 atm pressure, 1 kg of water), even though no salts or chemicals were added, there are tiny amounts of charged particles (ions) present from the natural splitting of water molecules. What is the ionic strength (measured in moles per kilogram of water) of this pure water at 25°C?

a) 1.26 × 10⁻⁵ mol/kgw  
b) 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
c) 1.36 × 10⁻⁹ mol/kgw  
d) 0.5 mol/kgw

In 1 kilogram and 1 atm pure water at 25°C, what are the concentrations of H⁺ ions and OH⁻ ions?

a) 1.008 × 10⁻⁶ mol/kgw each  
b) 1.007 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw each  
c) 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw each  
d) 1.201 × 10⁻⁹ mol/kgw each

You have pure water heated to 100°C, at normal atmospheric pressure (1 atm) with 1 kg of pure water with no added chemicals. What is the pH of pure water when heated to 100°C at 1 atm pressure?

a) 7.564  
b) 7.023  
c) 6.119  
d) 5.522

Comparing two identical samples of pure water (each 1 kg, no added chemicals, at 1 atm pressure) - one kept at room temperature (25°C) and one heated to boiling (100°C). When pure water is heated from 25°C to 100°C, how does its ionic strength change?

a) It decreases from 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ to 5.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw  
b) It remains constant at 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
c) It increases from 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ to 7.610 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw 
d) It increases to 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ mol/kgw

At 100°C and atmospheric pressure (1 atm), pure water (1 kg, no added substances) contains hydrogen ions (H⁺) and hydroxide ions (OH⁻) that formed from water molecules splitting apart. What is the concentration of H⁺ ions in pure water at 100°C?

a) 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
b) 7.610 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
c) 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ mol/kgw  
d) 1.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw

A series of measurements were taken on pure water (1 kg samples, no added chemicals, all at 1 atm pressure) at different temperatures ranging from freezing (0°C) to boiling (100°C), with measurements taken every 5 degrees. Each sample reached equilibrium at its specific temperature before measurements were recorded. At each temperature, the pH, ion concentrations, and other properties were measured. When pure water is held at 85°C (at 1 atm pressure), what is its pH?

a) 6.997  
b) 6.247  
c) 6.119  
d) 7.000

At a temperature study, pure water samples (each 1 kg, no additives, 1 atm pressure) were examined at various temperatures from 0°C to 100°C in 5-degree steps. At 90°C, the water has reached a stable state where the rate of water molecules splitting into ions equals the rate of ions recombining into water molecules. At 90°C, what is the ionic strength of pure water?

a) 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
b) 5.665 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
c) 6.281 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
d) 7.610 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw

System Description:** In the systematic temperature study from 0°C to 100°C, pure water (1 kg, no dissolved substances, 1 atm pressure) was analyzed at 95°C - very close to the boiling point. What is the concentration of OH⁻ ions in pure water at 95°C?

a) 6.281 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
b) 7.610 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
c) 5.665 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
d) 6.930 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw

A comprehensive study measured the properties of pure water at 21 different temperatures, evenly spaced from 0°C (freezing) to 100°C (boiling), all at normal atmospheric pressure (1 atm). Each sample contained 1 kg of pure water with no chemicals added. At each temperature point, the system was allowed to reach equilibrium before the pH was measured. The pH values observed were: 6.997 at 25°C, 6.247 at 85°C, and 6.119 at 100°C, among others.
Looking at the complete data set from 0°C to 100°C, what pattern emerges regarding the relationship between temperature and pH in pure water?

a) pH remains constant at 7.0 regardless of temperature  
b) pH increases as temperature increases  
c) pH decreases as temperature increases 
d) pH first increases then decreases with temperature

You have 1 kilogram of pure water at 25°C and 1 atm pressure. The pH has been set to 4.0. What is the electrical balance (difference between positive and negative charges) in equivalents?

a) 0 eq  
b) 5.041 × 10⁻⁵ eq  
c) 1.008 × 10⁻⁴ eq  
d) 1.012 × 10⁻⁴ eq

In 1 kilogram of water at 25°C and 1 atm with pH set to 4.0, what is the specific conductance of the solution?

a) 0 µS/cm  
b) 18 µS/cm  
c) 35 µS/cm  
d) 42 µS/cm

You have pure water at 25°C and 1 atm with pH 4.0. What is the concentration of OH⁻ ions in this acidic solution?

a) 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
b) 1.036 × 10⁻¹⁰ mol/kgw  
c) 1.021 × 10⁻¹⁰ mol/kgw  
d) 1.024 × 10⁻¹⁰ mol/kgw

You have 1 kilogram of water at 25°C and 1 atm. Chloride (Cl⁻) has been added at a concentration of 1.000 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw. The pH is calculated to balance the charges in the solution. What is the total alkalinity of this solution?

a) 0 eq/kg  
b) -1.000 × 10⁻⁴ eq/kg  
c) 1.000 × 10⁻⁴ eq/kg  
d) -1.012 × 10⁻⁴ eq/kg

Water at 25°C and 1 atm contains 1.000 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw of chloride ions (Cl⁻). After charge balancing, what is the percent error in the electrical balance calculation?

a) 100.00%  
b) 0.05%  
c) 0.00%  
d) -0.00%

In 1 kg of water at 25°C with 1.000 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw chloride, what is the activity (effective concentration) of H⁺ ions?

a) 1.000 × 10⁻⁴  
b) 1.008 × 10⁻⁴  
c) 9.887 × 10⁻⁵  
d) 1.012 × 10⁻⁴

You have 1 kg of water at 25°C and 1 atm. The pH is set to 4.0, and the amount of chloride (Cl⁻) is adjusted to balance the electrical charges in the solution. What is the final concentration of chloride needed to achieve charge balance?

a) 1.000 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
b) 1.012 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
c) 1.008 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
d) 5.041 × 10⁻⁵ mol/kgw

Water at 25°C has pH 4.0 with chloride concentration of 1.012 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw (charge balanced). What is the activity (effective concentration) of Cl⁻ ions?

a) 1.012 × 10⁻⁴  
b) 1.000 × 10⁻⁴  
c) 9.998 × 10⁻⁵  
d) 9.885 × 10⁻⁵

You start with 1 kg of pure water at 25°C and 1 atm (pH initially 6.997). Then 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ moles of hydrochloric acid (HCl) is added to the water. What is the final concentration of Cl⁻ ions after the reaction?

a) 0 mol/kgw  
b) 5.0 × 10⁻⁵ mol/kgw  
c) 1.000 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
d) 2.000 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw

Pure water at 25°C starts with specific conductance of 0 µS/cm. After adding 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ moles of HCl (which dissociates into H⁺ and Cl⁻), what is the new specific conductance?

a) 0 µS/cm  
b) 35 µS/cm  
c) 42 µS/cm  
d) 50 µS/cm

You have 1 kg of pure water at 25°C and 1 atm. When 0.1 moles of HCl is added to this water, what is the final pH?

a) 1.500  
b) 1.083  
c) 2.039  
d) 0.950

Starting with pure water at 25°C, you add 0.1 moles of HCl. What is the specific conductance of the resulting solution?

a) 4128 µS/cm  
b) 42 µS/cm  
c) 38961 µS/cm  
d) 412 µS/cm

Pure water at 25°C has 0.01 moles of HCl added to it. What is the pH of the solution after the addition?

a) 1.083  
b) 2.039  
c) 3.026  
d) 4.005

When 0.01 moles of HCl is added to 1 kg of pure water at 25°C, what is the activity of water in the solution?

a) 0.997  
b) 0.995  
c) 1.000  
d) 1.003

You add 1.0 × 10⁻³ moles of HCl to 1 kg of pure water at 25°C. What is the ionic strength of the resulting solution?

a) 1.0 × 10⁻² mol/kgw  
b) 1.0 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw  
c) 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
d) 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ mol/kgw

Pure water at 25°C receives an addition of 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ moles of HCl. What is the total alkalinity of this solution?

a) 0 eq/kg  
b) 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ eq/kg  
c) -1.0 × 10⁻⁴ eq/kg  
d) -1.0 × 10⁻³ eq/kg

When 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ moles of HCl is added to 1 kg of pure water at 25°C, what is the resulting pH?

a) 6.997  
b) 5.995  
c) 6.007  
d) 4.005

You add 1.0 × 10⁻⁸ moles of HCl to 1 kg of pure water at 25°C (initial pH 6.997). What is the final pH?

a) 6.997  
b) 6.976  
c) 6.950  
d) 7.010

Starting with pure water at 25°C with ionic strength 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw, you add 1.0 × 10⁻⁹ moles of HCl. What is the new ionic strength?

a) 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
b) 1.011 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
c) 1.100 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
d) 2.000 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw

You add 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁰ moles of HCl to 1 kg of pure water at 25°C. What is the H⁺ ion concentration after this very small addition?

a) 1.006 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
b) 1.007 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
c) 1.100 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw  
d) 1.000 × 10⁻¹⁰ mol/kgw

You have seawater at 25°C with the following composition: Ca 412.3 ppm, Mg 1291.8 ppm, Na 10768.0 ppm, K 399.1 ppm, Fe 0.002 ppm, Mn 0.0002 ppm, Si 4.28 ppm, Cl 19353.0 ppm, Alkalinity 141.682 ppm as HCO₃⁻, S(6) 2712.0 ppm, N(5) 0.29 ppm as NO₃⁻, N(-3) 0.03 ppm as NH₄⁺, pH 8.22, pe 8.451, and density 1.023 g/cm³. The water is equilibrated with O₂(g) at partial pressure 10⁻⁰·⁷ atm. What is the ionic strength of this seawater?

a) 0.485 mol/kgw  
b) 0.566 mol/kgw  
c) 0.6745 mol/kgw  
d) 0.750 mol/kgw

Seawater at 25°C contains Ca 412.3 ppm, Mg 1291.8 ppm, Na 10768.0 ppm, K 399.1 ppm, Cl 19353.0 ppm, Alkalinity 141.682 ppm as HCO₃⁻, S(6) 2712.0 ppm, pH 8.22, pe 8.451, density 1.023 g/cm³, and is equilibrated with O₂(g) at 10⁻⁰·⁷ atm. What is the total carbon (total CO₂) concentration in this seawater?

a) 1.480 × 10⁻³ mol/kg  
b) 2.177 × 10⁻³ mol/kg  
c) 2.406 × 10⁻³ mol/kg  
d) 3.739 × 10⁻⁵ mol/kg

Seawater at 25°C has the following composition: Ca 412.3 ppm, Mg 1291.8 ppm, Na 10768.0 ppm, K 399.1 ppm, Cl 19353.0 ppm, Alkalinity 141.682 ppm as HCO₃⁻, S(6) 2712.0 ppm, pH 8.22, density 1.023 g/cm³. What is the activity of water in this solution?

a) 1.000  
b) 0.997  
c) 0.981  
d) 0.950

Seawater at 25°C, pH 8.22, pe 8.451 contains total calcium of 1.066 × 10⁻² mol/kgw (from 412.3 ppm Ca), along with Mg 1291.8 ppm, Na 10768.0 ppm, K 399.1 ppm, Cl 19353.0 ppm, Alkalinity 141.682 ppm as HCO₃⁻, S(6) 2712.0 ppm. The calcium exists in different chemical forms including Ca²⁺, CaSO₄, CaHCO₃⁺, and CaCO₃. What is the molality of the free calcium ion (Ca²⁺)?

a) 1.066 × 10⁻² mol/kgw  
b) 9.531 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw  
c) 1.060 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw  
d) 2.658 × 10⁻⁵ mol/kgw

Seawater at 25°C, pH 8.22 contains Alkalinity 141.682 ppm as HCO₃⁻ (total alkalinity 2.406 × 10⁻³ eq/kg), total carbon 2.177 × 10⁻³ mol/kg, along with Ca 412.3 ppm, Mg 1291.8 ppm, Na 10768.0 ppm, Cl 19353.0 ppm, S(6) 2712.0 ppm. The carbonate species include HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, H₂CO₃, MgHCO₃⁺, NaHCO₃, CaHCO₃⁺, and others. What is the molality of bicarbonate ion (HCO₃⁻)?

a) 2.177 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw  
b) 1.480 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw  
c) 2.551 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
d) 3.739 × 10⁻⁵ mol/kgw

Seawater at 25°C, pH 8.22, pe 8.451 contains Ca 412.3 ppm, Mg 1291.8 ppm, Na 10768.0 ppm, K 399.1 ppm, Cl 19353.0 ppm, Alkalinity 141.682 ppm as HCO₃⁻, S(6) 2712.0 ppm, density 1.023 g/cm³. The water is equilibrated with atmospheric O₂(g) at a partial pressure of 10⁻⁰·⁷ atm (about 0.2 atm). Total dissolved oxygen content is 4.377 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw. What is the molality of dissolved O₂ gas?

a) 4.377 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
b) 2.556 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
c) 2.189 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
d) 3.849 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw

Seawater at 25°C contains Ca 412.3 ppm, Mg 1291.8 ppm, Na 10768.0 ppm, K 399.1 ppm, Fe 0.002 ppm, Mn 0.0002 ppm, Si 4.28 ppm, Cl 19353.0 ppm, Alkalinity 141.682 ppm as HCO₃⁻, S(6) 2712.0 ppm, N(5) 0.29 ppm as NO₃⁻, N(-3) 0.03 ppm as NH₄⁺, pH 8.22, pe 8.451, density 1.023 g/cm³. The electrical balance compares total cations to total anions to check the completeness of the analysis. What is the percent error in the charge balance?

a) 0.00%  
b) 0.07%  
c) 0.15%  
d) 1.00%

You have pure water at 25°C, pH 7.0, with no dissolved salts or minerals. The water is equilibrated with atmospheric O₂(g) at a partial pressure of 10⁻⁰·⁷ atm. The resulting solution has total O(0) content of 5.113 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw, ionic strength 1.001 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw, and activity of water is 1.000. What is the molality of dissolved O₂ gas?

a) 5.113 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
b) 2.556 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
c) 2.189 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
d) 3.849 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw

Pure water at 5°C (cold temperature), pH 7.0, with no dissolved salts, is equilibrated with atmospheric O₂(g) at a partial pressure of 10⁻⁰·⁷ atm (same as the 25°C case). The resulting solution has total O(0) content of 7.697 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw, ionic strength 5.928 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw, and activity of water is 1.000. What is the molality of dissolved O₂ gas?

a) 7.697 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
b) 2.556 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
c) 5.113 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw  
d) 3.849 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw

Two samples of pure water (pH 7.0, no dissolved salts) are equilibrated with O₂(g) at the same partial pressure of 10⁻⁰·⁷ atm. Sample 1 is at 25°C with dissolved O₂ molality of 2.556 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw. Sample 2 is at 5°C with dissolved O₂ molality of 3.849 × 10⁻⁴ mol/kgw. What is the ratio of O₂ solubility at 5°C to O₂ solubility at 25°C?

a) 1.00   
b) 0.66  
c) 1.51  
d) 2.00

You have a beaker with 1 kg of water at 25°C containing 1.0 mmol/kgw total dissolved inorganic carbon C(4), 300 mmol/kgw Cs⁺ (cesium chloride background electrolyte that does not form complexes), and 300 mmol/kgw Cl⁻. The pH is initially adjusted to 3.0. At this low pH, the carbonate system exists in different chemical forms: CO2 (dissolved carbon dioxide), HCO3- (bicarbonate), and CO3-2 (carbonate). What is the dominant carbon species and its molality at pH 3.0?

a) HCO3- at 1.0 × 10-3 mol/kgw  
b) CO2 at 9.993 × 10-4 mol/kgw  
c) CO3-2 at 8.153 × 10-14 mol/kgw  
d) HCO3- at 6.533 × 10-7 mol/kgw

The same beaker containing 1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 300 mmol/kgw Cs+, 300 mmol/kgw Cl-, at 25°C is titrated with KOH. After adding 2.1 mmol of KOH, the solution reaches pH 6.848. At this near-neutral pH, the carbonate speciation has changed significantly. Total C(4) is still 1.0 mmol/kgw distributed among CO2, HCO3-, and CO3-2. The ionic strength is 3.008 × 10-1 mol/kgw. What is the molality of bicarbonate (HCO3-) at pH 6.848?

a) 1.783 × 10-4 mol/kgw  
b) 7.216 × 10-7 mol/kgw  
c) 8.209 × 10-4 mol/kgw  
d) 1.0 × 10-3 mol/kgw

Continuing the KOH titration of the solution containing 1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 300 mmol/kgw Cs+, 300 mmol/kgw Cl- at 25°C, the pH reaches 7.245 after adding 2.2 mmol KOH. At this pH, total alkalinity is 9.223 × 10-4 eq/kg, ionic strength is 3.009 × 10-1 mol/kgw. The carbonate species present are CO2 (7.990 × 10-5 mol/kgw), HCO3- (9.181 × 10-4 mol/kgw), and CO3-2 (2.015 × 10-6 mol/kgw). What is the molality of dissolved CO2 at this pH?

a) 1.783 × 10-4 mol/kgw  
b) 7.990 × 10-5 mol/kgw  
c) 9.993 × 10-4 mol/kgw  
d) 1.421 × 10-7 mol/kgw

The solution containing 1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 300 mmol/kgw Cs+, 300 mmol/kgw Cl- at 25°C has been titrated to pH 9.785 by adding 2.7 mmol KOH. At this alkaline pH, the ionic strength is 3.019 × 10-1 mol/kgw, total alkalinity is 1.522 × 10-3 eq/kg. The three carbonate species are HCO3- (5.672 × 10-4 mol/kgw), CO3-2 (4.326 × 10-4 mol/kgw), and CO2 (1.421 × 10-7 mol/kgw). What is the molality of carbonate ion (CO3-2) at this pH?

a) 5.672 × 10-4 mol/kgw  
b) 1.0 × 10-3 mol/kgw  
c) 1.421 × 10-7 mol/kgw  
d) 4.326 × 10-4 mol/kgw

At very high pH (pH 11.517), the solution containing 1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 300 mmol/kgw Cs+, 300 mmol/kgw Cl-, 25°C has had 8.1 mmol KOH added. The ionic strength is 3.078 × 10-1 mol/kgw, total alkalinity is 6.822 × 10-3 eq/kg. At this high pH, the carbonate species are distributed as: CO3-2 (9.763 × 10-4 mol/kgw), HCO3- (2.366 × 10-5 mol/kgw), and CO2 (1.097 × 10-10 mol/kgw). What is the dominant carbonate species at pH 11.517?

a) HCO3- at 2.366 × 10-5 mol/kgw  
b) CO2 at 1.097 × 10-10 mol/kgw  
c) CO3-2 at 9.763 × 10-4 mol/kgw  
d) All three species are present in equal amounts

The beaker initially contains 1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 300 mmol/kgw Cs+, 300 mmol/kgw Cl- at pH 3.0 and 25°C. The ionic strength at the start is 3.000 × 10-1 mol/kgw. After titrating to pH 11.517 by adding 8.1 mmol KOH, the ionic strength becomes 3.078 × 10-1 mol/kgw. How much does the ionic strength change during the entire pH 3 to pH 11.5 titration?

a) Decreases from 0.300 to 0.250 mol/kgw  
b) Increases from 0.300 to 0.3078 mol/kgw  
c) Remains constant at 0.300 mol/kgw  
d) Increases from 0.300 to 0.450 mol/kgw

At pH 3.0, the solution contains 1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 298.7 mmol/kgw Cs+ (charge balanced), 300 mmol/kgw Cl-, 25°C. The activity of water is 0.990 and the ionic strength is 0.300 mol/kgw. The dominant carbonate species is CO2 with molality 9.993 × 10-4 mol/kgw but activity of 1.071 × 10-3. Why is the activity of CO2 higher than its molality?

a) Measurement error in the simulation  
b) CO2 is a neutral molecule with an activity coefficient greater than 1 (gamma = +0.030)  
c) High ionic strength always increases activity  
d) CO2 forms complexes that increase its activity

During the titration from pH 3 to pH 7.245, the solution (1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 300 mmol/kgw CsCl background electrolyte, 25°C) changes from being dominated by CO2 to being dominated by HCO3-. At pH 7.245, after adding 2.2 mmol KOH, the total alkalinity is 9.223 × 10-4 eq/kg, ionic strength 3.009 × 10-1 mol/kgw. How much KOH was needed to titrate from pH 3.0 (initial) to pH 7.245?

a) 1.0 mmol  
b) 2.2 mmol  
c) 8.1 mmol  
d) 0.1 mmol

At pH 11.517, the solution contains 1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 300 mmol/kgw Cs+, 300 mmol/kgw Cl-, 8.1 mmol/kgw K+ (from KOH addition), 25°C. The hydroxide ion (OH-) concentration is 4.846 × 10-3 mol/kgw and the H+ concentration is 3.895 × 10-12 mol/kgw. The total alkalinity is 6.822 × 10-3 eq/kg. The ionic strength is 3.078 × 10-1 mol/kgw. What is the molality of OH- at this high pH?

a) 3.895 × 10-12 mol/kgw  
b) 1.0 × 10-3 mol/kgw  
c) 4.846 × 10-3 mol/kgw  
d) 8.1 × 10-3 mol/kgw

Comparing the carbonate speciation at three different pH values in the same solution (1.0 mmol/kgw C(4), 300 mmol/kgw CsCl, 25°C):
- pH 3.0: CO2 = 9.993×10-4, HCO3- = 6.533×10-7, CO3-2 = 8.153×10-14 mol/kgw
- pH 7.245: CO2 = 7.990×10-5, HCO3- = 9.181×10-4, CO3-2 = 2.015×10-6 mol/kgw  
- pH 11.517: CO2 = 1.097×10-10, HCO3- = 2.366×10-5, CO3-2 = 9.763×10-4 mol/kgw

Which statement correctly describes the dominant carbonate species across this pH range?

a) CO2 dominates at all pH values  
b) CO3-2 dominates at all pH values  
c) CO2 dominates at pH 3, HCO3- dominates at pH 7.2, and CO3-2 dominates at pH 11.5  
d) HCO3- dominates at all three pH values

You have 1 kg of water at 25°C containing trace amounts of ions: Al 1.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw, Ca 2.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw, Cl 7.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw, K 1.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw, Na 1.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw, S 1.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw, and O(0) 1.0 × 10⁻⁶ mol/kgw. The pH is 6.915 (charge balanced), ionic strength is 2.122 × 10⁻⁷ mol/kgw. Then 0.001 mol KCl is added, increasing the ionic strength to 1.000 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw. At this ionic strength, the monovalent cation K⁺ has molality 1.000 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw and activity 9.648 × 10⁻⁴. What is the activity coefficient (gamma) of K⁺ at ionic strength 0.001 M?

a) 1.000  
b) 0.965  
c) 0.850  
d) 0.700

The same solution at 25°C now contains K 1.000 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw, Cl 1.000 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw, Ca 2.0 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw (as Ca²⁺), trace amounts of other ions, pH 6.917, ionic strength 1.000 × 10⁻³ mol/kgw. The divalent cation Ca²⁺ has molality 2.000 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw and activity 1.737 × 10⁻⁸. What is the log gamma value for Ca²⁺ at this ionic strength?

a) -0.016  
b) -0.061  
c) -0.133  
d) 0.000

After adding more KCl to reach 0.1 mol/kgw K and 0.1 mol/kgw Cl at 25°C (ionic strength 1.000 × 10⁻¹ mol/kgw, pH 6.915, water activity 0.997), the monovalent cation K⁺ has molality 1.000 × 10⁻¹ mol/kgw and activity 6.947 × 10⁻² (log gamma = -0.158). The divalent cation Ca²⁺ (molality 2.000 × 10⁻⁸ mol/kgw) has activity 8.051 × 10⁻⁹. What is the log gamma for Ca²⁺ at ionic strength 0.1 M?

a) -0.061  
b) -0.158  
c) -0.395  
d) -0.800

Comparing activity coefficients at different ionic strengths for the monovalent ion Cl⁻ at 25°C:
- I = 0.001 M: Cl⁻ molality = 1.000 × 10⁻³, activity = 9.649 × 10⁻⁴, log gamma = -0.016
- I = 0.1 M: Cl⁻ molality = 1.000 × 10⁻¹, activity = 7.737 × 10⁻², log gamma = -0.111
